---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size45[
What are the Complicating Factors in Environmental Policymaking?

+ Unequal Voice, Access and Influence
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Prep email with link to Google Doc for citations

???

Let's cut through my fancy language here.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-climate_change_is_racism.webp')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: top

???

This week I'd like us to explore how the role of race and racism complicates our efforts to solve environmental problems.

Specifically, I'd like us to consider a particular argument.

SLIDE

--

.size35[.center[.content-box-green[**Are we convinced that the American environmental**]]]

.size35[.center[.content-box-green[**movement is built on and perpetuates racist**]]]

.size35[.center[.content-box-green[**outcomes?**]]]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-tragedy_of_the_tragedy.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

WHAT IS THE CENTRAL ARGUMENT?

<br>

WHAT ARE THE KEY PREMISES SUPPORTING THIS CONCLUSION?

<br>

Let's examine the sections of Hardin's article that Mildenberger flags as problematic.

WHAT IS HARDIN'S ARGUMENT IN THE SECTION THE "FREEDOM TO BREED IS INTOLERABLE"?

WHAT IS HARDIN'S ARGUMENT ABOUT PROVIDING FOOD AID TO DEVELOPING COUNTRIES?

WHAT IS HARDIN'S ARGUMENT ABOUT THE UN AND THE DECLARATION ON HUMAN RIGHTS?

<br>

SO, IS MILDENBERGER'S ARGUMENT CONVINCING? WHY OR WHY NOT?

<br>

Hardin's environmental concerns were driven almost entirely by his racism and disdain for the poor.

+ Promoted “lifeboat ethics”: since global resources are finite, Hardin believed the rich should throw poor people overboard to keep their boat above water.

+ He argues the “freedom to breed is intolerable” and that we should let “children of improvident parents starve to death” so the poor would have fewer kids. 

+ He opposed sending food aid to poor nations, because he believed their populations were threatening Earth’s “carrying capacity."

A bad dude using concerns about environmental scarcity to justify racial discrimination.

<br>

So, here's where I go back to my conflict in assigning this piece.

I think Hardin's pasture example helps us better understand collective action problems and free-riding, but I want you to know:

He was wrong.

The history of the commons he invokes didn't exist.

As Susan Cox pointed out, early pastures were well regulated by local institutions. 

+ They were not free-for-all grazing sites where people took and took at the expense of everyone else.

And as Elinor Ostrom showed with her NOBEL PRIZE WINNING WORK many global commons have been similarly sustained through community institutions.

+ We'll study some of them in week 13 of this term!

<br>

Ok, so, Hardin was an a**-hat, but his hypothetical is a useful way to think about individuals making individually rational but collectively sub-optimal choices.

FAIR ENOUGH?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-kashwan.png')
background-size: 90%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Kashwan, P. (2020, September 2). American environmentalism’s racist roots have shaped global thinking about conservation. *The Conversation*.

WHAT IS THE CENTRAL ARGUMENT?

WHAT ARE THE KEY PREMISES SUPPORTING THIS CONCLUSION?

IS IT CONVINCING? WHY OR WHY NOT?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[
Bullard, R. D., Mohai, P., Saha, R., & Wright, B. (2007). Toxic Wastes and Race at Twenty: Why Race Still Matters After All of These Years. United Church of Christ Justice & Witness Ministries. (**ONLY ch 3 and 4**)
]

???

WHAT IS THE CENTRAL ARGUMENT IN CHAPTERS 3 AND 4?

WHAT ARE THE KEY PREMISES SUPPORTING THIS CONCLUSION?

IS IT CONVINCING? WHY OR WHY NOT?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-env_justice.jpg')
background-size: 86%
background-position: center
class: top

???

OK, WHERE DO WE END UP AT THIS POINT?

- ARE WE CONVINCED THAT THE AMERICAN ENVIRONMENTAL MOVEMENT IS BUILT ON AND PERPETUATES RACIST OUTCOMES?

*DISCUSS*

<br>

Ok, let's use our case study exercise this week to help us keep attacking this question!

SLIDE


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

.size60[**For Thursday: Environmental Racism Cases**]

.size40[
Find us a **recent, real world example** of an environmental problem being framed in terms of its **disparate impact on a minority group**.

Be ready to help us **brainstorm strategies** for addressing these complications.
]

???

You don't have to find a case that mirrors the problem you've selected for your paper.

HOWEVER, that's not a bad idea!

My aim is that we broaden and deepen our understanding of risk profiles complicating environmental problem-solving.

So, go find us good cases we can dig our teeth into.

**Submit the citation to your case before class.**

Be ready to present your case to the class AND start thinking about strategies we can use to address these risk based problems.

QUESTIONS?
